items.prompt_md: In **post-order** traversal, when is a node's own work done relative to its two recursive calls?
options: Between the left and right recursive calls
After both recursive calls have returned
Only if the node is a leaf
items.answer: After both recursive calls have returned
items.explanation_md: Post-order means 'children first, then the node' — you recurse into left and right, and only once both have returned do you touch the current node's own result.

items.prompt_md: You must transform or accumulate over every node of a binary tree exactly once, and the work at each node only depends on results from its own children. Which approach fits?
options: Depth-first recursion, doing the node's own work before or after recursing into its children
Sort all the node values into an array and scan it once
Two pointers converging from the tree's leftmost and rightmost leaves
A fixed-size sliding window over the node values
items.answer: Depth-first recursion, doing the node's own work before or after recursing into its children
items.explanation_md: The tree's own recursive definition — a node plus two subtrees — is the solution's structure. Whenever a node's work only needs its children's results, pre/post-order DFS recursion is the direct fit.

items.prompt_md: A recursive DFS visits every node of an n-node binary tree exactly once. In terms of n and the tree's height h, what is its time and space complexity?
options: O(n) time, O(h) space for the recursion stack
O(n log n) time, O(1) space
O(n) time, O(n) space regardless of the tree's shape
items.answer: O(n) time, O(h) space for the recursion stack
items.explanation_md: Every node does O(1) work once, so the pass is O(n) time. The only extra memory is the call stack, which holds at most one frame per level of depth — O(h), which ranges from O(log n) balanced to O(n) for a skewed chain.
